channel.title: Cathode Ray Dude Gaiden
channel.description: This is a side channel for livestreams, weird side projects, and stuff that will screw up the stats for my main channel, Cathode Ray Dude.
channel.itunes:author: Cathode Ray Dude Gaiden
channel.itunes:subtitle: Cathode Ray Dude Gaiden
channel.itunes:summary: This is a side channel for livestreams, weird side projects, and stuff that will screw up the stats for my main channel, Cathode Ray Dude.

item.title: DEC VXT2000 connecting to Red Hat 6.1
item.description:
I wanted to play xevil on the VXT, but I wasn't sure if it could talk to something new enough to have a prebuilt package. The terminal came out around 1992, so I decided to stand up Red Hat 6.1 (1999) and see if it would talk. The answer is a qualified yes.
As with the last test, it takes an extremely long time to connect. I'm not sure why; I need to look at wireshark and see if it's swapping hard to the InfoServer, that could explain it. Certainly it's not a lack of local processing power because once the session is up and running it kinda goes like gangbusters, so maybe upgrading the RAM in the VXT will alleviate this. Kind of defeats the purpose of Server Mode, but what can you do, RAM used to be $$$.
As you can see, it connects, gets a GDM banner, actually picks up the *entire* Gnome 1 environment (unlike HP-UX CDE which failed to spawn the panel and desktop) and is able to launch apps. It takes forever to start each app, but then they run remarkably well! GIMP explodes due to some kind of weird GIMP nonsense (it seems to spawn 500,000 windows telling me it can't access swap; I don't think that's the VXTs fault) but check out how fast it transfers that gradient across the 10baseT. Likewise, Netscape takes a while to start, but it's LIGHTNING fast scrolling through my website even with a bunch of big images. So again, I'm convinced the slow startup has nothing to do with CPU or network speed.
The fonts are mostly trashed and I suspect that's due to X not finding a font and falling back to something inappropriate. I need to see if XFS is running and connecting to resolve this.
I should note that this is being captured off the VXT2000's monitor port through an Extron scaler, so this is the actual performance of a non-upgraded 1992 X terminal - but from what I've read, the VXT2000 is *exceptional* in that regard, and most at the time were much slower.
item.itunes:author: Cathode Ray Dude Gaiden
item.itunes:subtitle: DEC VXT2000 connecting to Red Hat 6.1
item.itunes:summary:
I wanted to play xevil on the VXT, but I wasn't sure if it could talk to something new enough to have a prebuilt package. The terminal came out around 1992, so I decided to stand up Red Hat 6.1 (1999) and see if it would talk. The answer is a qualified yes.
As with the last test, it takes an extremely long time to connect. I'm not sure why; I need to look at wireshark and see if it's swapping hard to the InfoServer, that could explain it. Certainly it's not a lack of local processing power because once the session is up and running it kinda goes like gangbusters, so maybe upgrading the RAM in the VXT will alleviate this. Kind of defeats the purpose of Server Mode, but what can you do, RAM used to be $$$.
As you can see, it connects, gets a GDM banner, actually picks up the *entire* Gnome 1 environment (unlike HP-UX CDE which failed to spawn the panel and desktop) and is able to launch apps. It takes forever to start each app, but then they run remarkably well! GIMP explodes due to some kind of weird GIMP nonsense (it seems to spawn 500,000 windows telling me it can't access swap; I don't think that's the VXTs fault) but check out how fast it transfers that gradient across the 10baseT. Likewise, Netscape takes a while to start, but it's LIGHTNING fast scrolling through my website even with a bunch of big images. So again, I'm convinced the slow startup has nothing to do with CPU or network speed.
The fonts are mostly trashed and I suspect that's due to X not finding a font and falling back to something inappropriate. I need to see if XFS is running and connecting to resolve this.
I should note that this is being captured off the VXT2000's monitor port through an Extron scaler, so this is the actual performance of a non-upgraded 1992 X terminal - but from what I've read, the VXT2000 is *exceptional* in that regard, and most at the time were much slower.

item.title: DEC VXT200 boot process
item.description:
I spent the last couple days getting a DEC VXT200 X terminal set up, cabled and bootable. It's much, much harder than you'd think and involves an absurd rigamarole of setting up multiple emulated DEC machines including a VAX and InfoServer in order to get the *correct* boot server setup. There's no way to simplify the process, because this thing actually uses the InfoServer as *swap space* due to its extremely limited local RAM. You can run it in "Host Mode" where it does not do this, but supposedly it's a worse experience so I haven't tried it yet.
Anyway, here's what it looks like to boot up from cold and connect to an HP-UX machine. Notice that it takes over six minutes to get into a session - I have no idea if that's normal, but I suspect it is for the era (~1994.)
Also, I don't know why the entire CDE session doesn't appear - the panel and desktop background don't show up, and there's no obvious way to get to an app launcher, so I suspect this is not the intended experience.
item.itunes:author: Cathode Ray Dude Gaiden
item.itunes:subtitle: DEC VXT200 boot process
item.itunes:summary:
I spent the last couple days getting a DEC VXT200 X terminal set up, cabled and bootable. It's much, much harder than you'd think and involves an absurd rigamarole of setting up multiple emulated DEC machines including a VAX and InfoServer in order to get the *correct* boot server setup. There's no way to simplify the process, because this thing actually uses the InfoServer as *swap space* due to its extremely limited local RAM. You can run it in "Host Mode" where it does not do this, but supposedly it's a worse experience so I haven't tried it yet.
Anyway, here's what it looks like to boot up from cold and connect to an HP-UX machine. Notice that it takes over six minutes to get into a session - I have no idea if that's normal, but I suspect it is for the era (~1994.)
Also, I don't know why the entire CDE session doesn't appear - the panel and desktop background don't show up, and there's no obvious way to get to an app launcher, so I suspect this is not the intended experience.
